entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Hi George, Would you mind telling me a little bit about your organization, who you are in the company and what your goal and mission is?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so yeah, we are the Green Haven project and essentially what we do is we try to create community farms in urban areas where they work as a resource center that's not just limited to providing free veggies, but we try to do back to school events, Christmas events, pretty much anything to make people's living experiences a little bit easier. And yeah, my position, I'm one of the co founders. I normally don't use this title but on paper I'm under as president. And I was trying to think what was the other. Because you end up editing this, right? Yeah. What was the other questions?

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So I guess when did you start and yeah. So what inspired you to start this type of initiative?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so we started, I want to say back in 2018 and it was the way it was started. It was with a few folks. Originally it was me and one of our other co founders by the name of David and he was invited to come to the space from one of our friends named Michael from Talent for Change that he knew that there's going to be a group of kids that were going to be here that we worked with at the Overtown Youth Center. He and invited him. David told me about it and we both equally had the mindset of trying to find our way to come in here and being able to do what we can to help and serve and do, you know, justice to the community and the area. And you know, from there we met Thais which she was one of the women that was originally on this project that, you know, in the beginning her and a few other folks were trying to do a production farm and she just couldn't handle it. It like one of those right place at the right time kind of thing. And she, she offered us the space and she introduced us tomorrow which then he was able to give the master lease to original which was under an organization called Save the Kids. And then eventually I want to say like maybe a year and a half later, we created the Green Haven project which was started with nine people. Currently it's about four folks that are on the right now, which is myself, Josh, also known as Legacy Placeres Takiya. Those are the board members and some of our other teammates that help us without is Selena and Bottles. Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And the five other folks that don't have their. What is their position?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: The other folks that I mentioned that helped found it, they're not with the org anymore. Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So since you started back in 2018, have you. What have been some of the hurdles that you've had to overcome, some of the challenges you faced, and have you had to pivot at all, any portion of your activities?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah. So I feel like there's been a multitude of things that have been a hurdle. One of the things that continues to be a hurdle, as you know, we're now going into like, maybe I believe it's our fifth year of being an official non profit is funding. And that funding becomes a hurdle. Not necessarily to be able to pay people out or anything, but more so for the sake of being able to obtain resources. We've been blessed and fortunate to partner with other orgs that have been involved and other like, generous donors that have at least been able to help us to keep up with a functional growing season. But that's been one thing. A lot of it has been some of the issue surrounding the neighborhood, which ends up being a lot of. We're surrounded by a lot of the drug epidemic where there's people that end up, you know, using, you know, substances and things like, around the property. And, you know, we've had situations where people have might have potentially like overdosed and you know, from there to like people being in a mindset of, you know, unfortunately being inebriated that ends up doing. Ends up having people do death, desperate things. So we've had unfortunately, like people that are on the streets that have broken into the property, stolen property, stolen like some materials and things like that that we have. So that's been difficult because it's like, it's. It's one of those things where justice was never served and then that ends up affecting us and we end up having to come out of pocket again to like replenish the resources and things that we've lost.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And how often does that happen where.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: It doesn't happen as often as before, because I mentioned before, we've had some funding with cra, which is the Community Redevelopment Agency of Overtown, that they gave us some fundings and we've recently redone the entire fence throughout their property. That's something that essentially we wouldn't have done. We wouldn't have invested so much money on a new fence or anything like that. But it was something we had to do because it was a reoccurring thing that like, throughout the years, like, it maybe might have happened where like, we've had a big break in like three to four times a year, like within like at least our first three to four years and even minor Attempts now, even though we should have the new fence.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So what is it that people are like they're breaking in and what are their, what are they stealing?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, sometimes they're just being the most random things right now. What, what has been stolen in the past was a lot of tools, a lot of equipments. We used to have old tool sheds that you know, we had a lot of gas powered equipments that were stolen. Such whatchamacallit like lawnmowers, weed whackers, things like that. You know, we've had wheelbarrow stolen. But then like I said, it ends up being a lot of random minor things like people have stolen bins and garbage bins and things like that too. And just things that doesn't make sense and unfortunately like not only does it hurts us, but it hurts the community as well.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: I actually want to go back to this portion you mentioned. You're the co founder and also the president. Can you describe your role on a day to day basis in.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so I mentioned before those are just titles that I don't necessarily like apply that to kind of hold a position. But what I end up doing in the org is I help to pretty much be the garden manager. So I help with, you know, coordinating with, you know, folks who donate plants and grow plants and for us to purchase them, such as our friends from Little River Co Op. And you know, I, I, I go about like, you know, organizing with the people who donate moch, the people who drop off soil, knowing how things are going to be planted, you know, helping to organize plant days and what grows here and the trees we plan and harvesting and all that too. So.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, awesome. So I want to talk a little bit about, you know, the, you mentioned funding was, was a challenge. But I do want to talk about what your funding structure looks like. How often are you raising funds? How are you able to sustain, you know, the day to day activities of Green Haven?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so green the way, honestly the way it's sustained, you know, even if there's a lack of funding is just passion because this isn't a job for any of us. There isn't like a payroll or anything like that that kind of like fluctuates where like if that's not happening, then we're not happening. You know, like we still come, we've been doing that from the beginning that we've come from our own whim and from just the, the heart of our own selves that we want to be here and be able to do that. So a lot of it's just making time. You know, there does ends up being a lot of burnt out, you know, because of that, where it's like, you know, a lot of us still have jobs and then we'll end up doing this, you know, on the side kind of thing. So essentially it's a passion project and that kind of keeps up with us being able to continue is that, you know, we have the will to do this despite money being involved or not. Does that answer? Yeah.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And so how many are you guys that are dedicating this time, your time on an active daily or weekly basis?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so on the board right now. Yeah, so on the board right now, there's at least, you know, three of us that dedicates differently. You know, like for example, one of our co founders, Takiya, she. She does all the paperwork. She does all that, like leg work that just completely. That like really puts us in check in, in a sense of like she got all the documents done, she got her taxes done, all those things, you know, God bless her, because that's something I don't want to do. That's something I wouldn't want to do. I don't do well with computers or the tech and I'm very awful at like answering texts and emails and stuff like that. So, you know, that does help or, you know, she's able to handle that. And then we got a few people that ends up being on the ground, such as myself, Legacy that I mentioned, which is another co founder, and then two of our other teammates, which is Bottles and Selena. They try to come when they can, but like I said, it's hard when this isn't necessarily a paying job for folks to try to commit to because other people have other obligations and commitment. And it's understandable. But fortunately we end up getting a lot of regulars and a lot of hands and, you know, just a lot of good folks that ends up wanting to be and continue with a lot of the project, which, you know, from the recorded footage that might, that you might have. You know, we have our friends Jack and Mark that are here and they've been pretty consistent. And you know, it's like folks we've known like within the last two to three years and they've been trying to come as much as they can. But those are just some examples that I share where it's like this becomes a community within itself, where people are part of the Green Haven community in that sense that, oh, they don't have to be a board member, they don't have to be an employee or anything. They just love the project and want to help. So we end up having a lot of people like that and yeah, shouts out to them.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: How big would you say is the community?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: That community that I speak of? It's hard to say because, like, it expands from, like, a lot of different. Like I said, just people coming on their own whims and then ends up being a lot of orgs. And that involves, like, a lot of people. Like, we have this one yoga group called Cafe Namaste, which is managed by Sam and a few other folks that, you know that she's been put. She's been working with us for like two to three years now. And it's been a. A regular thing where they've been coming now like once a month. And they'll end up bringing about like 15, 15 to 20 people at a time, sometimes even more. But that is in itself an example on how people pretty much come and be a part of that community where it's really hard to calculate, like, how big it is and how much it expands. And, you know, even as we're talking, there's like, more friends that are just like, walking in that. They're also a part of that too. So I honestly wouldn't be able to come up with a. And you wouldn't be able to use our social media as an example of those numbers either, because we have like, about like 10k following on. On Instagram. But that's not necessarily the Green Haven community because it's, you know, a lot of people that support in different ways, but they're not necessarily, like, around to say, like, oh, yeah, they're a part of that. Not to say that I wouldn't want them to be, but that's just the real realness of it. So.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Can you speak a little bit about, you know, the main activities that you guys do? Because I know you mentioned you guys have some community events, but then there's also a nursery and you guys also welcome a lot of volunteers. So I'd love to. Can you paint a picture for me of the different activities?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah. So a lot of what happens here, I'll just get the big events out the way first, which we end up having Back to School event, which we actually have next week with our friend Andrea and the Marv production. And Girl Talk Radio, they do that once a year. They have been doing that now, I want to say, for like the last six years or so with us. And we also have like a. They also help with a toy drive that happens every December as well. So those are like some minor events.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And then what are these events? What happens during.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yes. So for the back to school drive, you know, we end up having like, you know, just a few things that are out like for the kids to kind of play and hang out with. We might have like a water slide. I think they are bringing like some type of water based things for the kids to enjoy. But we end up having them hang out. We end up having a dj which I think recently the last years it might have been DJ EPS from Power 96 or 99 jams, I could be mistaken on those. And you know, it just ends up being a hangout. We end up having food and then kind of a certain time we have, you know, the family and the kids line up to collect and gather whatever resources they can for back to school. So they end up having a lot of generous donations from backpacks to markers and pens and things like that and journals. And then the, the Christmas drive is very similar where there's usually things for kids to kind of like indulge in. There's been years where they've even bought out like they rented a man who has a pony and kids were able to kind of like ride around that throughout the garden and stuff like that too. So it ends up being one thing and yeah, has like a similar structure where like at a certain point they end up giving out like all the, the things and stuff like that. And then.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So that was the event.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, the events portion. Some of the events, the ones that like come off right at the top of my head. But we try to do things such as like doing food drives, donation drives. Those donation drives usually consist of like, you know, clothes and other resources and stuff like that that we had worked with earlier with the South Florida Wellness center where they just bought a bunch of stuff from cooked food to clothes and things like that.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: That.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: And then, you know, you just kind of tell a few folks within the, the neighborhood and the community and then word spreads and everyone knows and people are just coming to grab things. That's usually when we do food drives as well. How they work out where it's like certain orgs will come, they'll bring a lot of food and they pretty much set up like in this structure that we're in and just fill up all the table with boxes and stuff. We go to a few doors, knock on, on them and let people know like, hey, we got things. And you know, we got like bags and stuff that are available for people to already like fill up their own Stuff. So that's. Those are some events that ends up happening. And then usually on a regular basis, when it comes to just functioning as just regular volunteers, we end up, you know, having a lot of people coming together just to kind of help to maintain the space. Like, right now in the summer, what we end up doing is just a lot of upkeep, because in the summer, it's usually pretty rough, where we have a lot of rain in summer, such as it's raining now and the weeds just fill this up. So we usually need a lot of hands and a lot of people to help to maintain the space. And when we have volunteers, we end up trying to bring a lot of the weeds down, try to do what we can to temporarily cover them until we get into the season, which once we're in season, we end up growing, you know, a lot of veggies, a lot of production. And from there, we pretty much have people helping to keep up with those garden rows, those veggies. With harvesting, sometimes we have events where we do, like, a big giveaway where we're posted up somewhere and we've bought a lot of the veggies that we harvested from the garden to that one location that ends up being kind of like the regular flow of when we're in season. So. Okay, awesome.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And can you talk about any major milestones or what are you the proudest of since you started, since you guys have started in 2018?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, I feel like a major milestone which isn't often recognized, that is that we. We're pretty much known as. As one of the one. Well, I mean, there are. I don't. I can't really say like that, but we are, like, one of the few community gardens that are open in the way that we are throughout South Florida, where most people that are known as a growing space, as a community gardener, all these things, they're not open. Like we are in a sense of, like, once we have our doors open, people know that they can come here, and there's isn't a lot of rules in terms of, like, what you can do on the space, in terms of, like, oh, can I go in there and harvest? Is that an issue? Can I go in there and explore? Can I bring my kids? Can I bring my dogs? You know, we're very proud to say that, like, this place has become a sanctuary for a lot of people, has become a. From this is the other people's testimony, has become a sense of therapy for people to just be able to come out here and have this garden available. To do that where not a lot of people have the space or the opportunity to be able to grow and produce and do all these things that, you know, for me, that's. That's like a major milestone. Like, you know, I don't necessarily look at like, records or numbers or awards or any things like that. Like, I tend to look at how it benefits and affects people in a positive light where, like, they can say that, oh yeah, I can go to Green Haven and it's that spot where I can just chill out and hang out and, you know, meet a lot of, like, mutual good people. And, you know, for it to be a portal like that has been very. A very positive thing. But, you know, we were to get, like, more intricate. You know, we've. We've been able to produce a lot. It's hard to keep up with how much we've actually produced and given out and had people, like, actually feel like this has been a place where I can get groceries where they normally don't have it, like that, you know, that in itself is a testimony for us that, you know, since COVID you know, we've been just at it. We haven't stopped where, like, we're just trying to do what we can to innovate and grow on what we're currently doing and expanding on that and just being able to do more. When it comes to food production, especially in a time where food scarcity is an inevitable thing, unfortunately.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: That is great. And what motivates you? Because I know you mentioned it's clearly not the money because this is not a paid, you know, position. So what motivates you and why did you want to provide a space, a sanctuary for others? What is it about that that drives you?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, for me, I feel like I think it's being a part of the goodness of the world. Because, you know, if you think of Miami culture, you don't often think of community gardens. You don't think of orgs that are doing things for the community. You think of more of like, the lavishness of Miami and, you know, like the party going and all this and the development and all those things. What keeps me going is to know that I am. I am part of the opposite aspect of. Of what. The opposite aspect of what Miami is known for. And, you know, I like being in a place where people recognize me as being a part of something that's good. And then from there, kind of, in a sense, people paint me as a good person too, and not to care of what people think of you, but you Know, realistically, in this world, like, you want to leave the best impression that you can. So, you know, I'm very proud to be, you know, in a place that creates just life in general. Life from food, from creating, you know, habitat that brings in other life that most people don't recognize, and then just all the. The good people that the space brings as well. I'm very grateful to be a. Proud of that. That keeps me motivated because, you know, there's a lot of things that happens, you know, here, you know, within the inner community, things like that, that can be very discouraging. But then I'm reminded, you know, from other people that, you know, because of you or because of us being here and this and that, that, it. It. It does bring, like, a sense of joy. So I think, you know, knowing that those testimonies exist, you know, keeps me motivated and wanting to move forward and to continue going. Even when times and things feel more difficult or it kind of feels like maybe the work is in vain, that there's, you know, a lot of love and honor that has came from that, and. And yeah, just being a part of that just makes me grateful.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And what are your goals for the future?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: The going for the future? So something that I've always had in mind when it comes to the space is being able to create employment. We kind of saw, like, a small fracture of that during the summer where we had a little bit of funding to work with, where we was able to, you know, pay out some folks in the community here and there to kind of like, help maintain. And, you know, that felt very good because there's a point where everything felt like it was flourishing. Everything around us, not just the garden, the space, but then the people who are benefiting from, you know, being able to receive an opportunity. That felt amazing, that felt very good. And, you know, eventually we want to be able to have funding more, to be more liquid, where we can do that more, where we can be able to employ people. And, you know, because I always have constantly people having and asking for work and, you know, it just sucks to be. Not be able to say yes. And, you know, that's something I see for the future, the. The space being restructured a little differently, where it's like everything is in its full capacity because we'll go like the years, we're like, all right, well, we got this down point, but then we're lacking on this part and lacking on that. So. So. And that's something too, where, you know, just being on point with everything as best as we possibly can, where everything still collectively feels like it's flourishing and growing in different ways and things like that too. You know, to see, like, a lot of projects be able to be completed, such as the nursery, our farm, like, having a more successful farming yield, having our chicken situation fixed situation, the chicken situations that we haven't been able to, like, really buckle down and get them, like, wrangled up to be in the chicken coop. It ends up being a lot of work, but that also requires someone to be committed to do that. Because I feel bad that we, all, right, we got the chickens in the coop, but we're not able to keep up with them where someone's, like, feeding them, they're getting water, and then we collect the eggs and things like that. So that's one thing. Getting our be situation more structured too, where. Where we have, like, beekeepers coming regularly, being able to teach beekeeping lessons and things like that for people that wants to learn, being able to get certifications and things like that. So just different things like that that comes to mind where it's like, dang, I wish we had this, this and that. But that's something that we're aiming to, you know, have completed within the next few months leading into 2026, I guess.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: In general, though, what would you say is. Is critical in terms of knowledge or in terms of skill to be able to successfully run this organization and maintain it?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, a lot of it is a lot of will to do it in a sense of, you know, you have to be. You have to have the will to keep up with people, to be able to manage your time, being able to manage other people, being able to manage the land. You know, a lot of it sounds like, oh, having a skill of management. But I see it as a skill of will because I feel like anyone can manage. But do you have the will to do it, the willpower to, you know, kind of rid yourself of the distractions and actually buckle down and focus A lot of it. Choose a lot of experience that, you know, people often ask, like, how do you start? Where do you start? And my main thing is just to advise people to get out there. Because you might not know what you're doing or what you want to do. But the. The main thing would always be to at least start somewhere.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: That's what you would recommend to others.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: To other people who hasn't figured out to at least find somewhere to get started, to go out and just be a part of something. If you don't know where to go, where to start. Just like. Yeah, just, just volunteer somewhere. You don't know who you'll ever meet, what experiences might come out of that and then go from there too. So.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: One second.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, what's up, y'? All? Sorry about that. Been in interview mode right now and it's also been raining too. You find something? Yeah, there's. There's star fruits. Yeah. Oh, what is this? Okay. Yeah, you got.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Now I'd love to talk mainly around like the different stakeholders of the business, but also like the different players. So you've talked a lot about the different partnerships that you have going on with the universities and you know, with one of them you're going to be building out the nursery. So can you talk to me me a little bit about what the relationship is like with different types of stakeholders? We can start with the universities.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. I want to say, you know, when it comes to like, you know, schools and other, you know, universities that come through the. The relationship pretty much functions where it's like a yearly thing, it feels like since we started where, you know, some people might have like specific events and things like that that they do throughout the year. I was trying to think. Yeah, I feel like, you know, with some of the relationships we've had with other people has been a little more regular. They tend to feel seasonal. Seasonal where there isn't something that's necessarily coming regularly. Except with how I mentioned with the Smile Trust that, you know, we've worked with them for a few years but as of recently we've been having a more regular thing where they've been coming every month to do a houseless feeding every third Sunday. So that's been a reoccurring thing with Cafe Namaste. They usually try to come every fourth Sunday of the month. Month. Bless you.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Thank you.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: And you know, that helps because there ends up being like more something that fluctuates and something that happens every month, especially like even in the summer when I mentioned it's a little slower.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And Cafe Namaste, it's also feeding.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: No, they, they help with the garden, they help with maintenance and doing that. Such as like these new rows that have been installed. They, you know, they've been doing. They. That was like one of the last project that they helped with. So yeah, that's something that happens monthly in terms of things that are regular. And then. Yeah, I mentioned things are a little more seasonal like with one of our partnerships with FIU has also been with I Care and Bank United. I Care ends up being a non profit, non profit sector. Of BankUnited.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And I'm sorry, so you mentioned FIU, is that, like, through FIU, you have a relationship with iCare or Bank Trust through them?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, we were able to meet. Yeah, iCare and BankUnited, and they've been helping us, like, at least for the last three years with additional fundings and helping us to get resources and things like that. Where every October we have, we celebrate World Food Day, which ends up happening, like, in the middle of October. And, you know, those ends up being a big event that kind of like, sets the tone for the rest of the season because we end up organizing. They end up being a group of people that, you know, we end up doing a lot of the crops and a lot of the garden rows that. That starts off the season.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, and from your perspective, like, for Smile Trust or even Cafe d', Maste, what do you think that they're getting from you?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: So for Smile Trust, for example, they have the opportunity to be able to utilize the space, which is very important for us, because, you know, we want this space to be used in a manner that practical for other people. Because, you know, it's like, sure, we can have our own operation, but if there are more people wanting to have with ideas and other opportunities to come and, you know, pretty much help innovate, not just a project, but, you know, and supporting the community, then you know, that's beneficial to us. But, you know, it helps them because it's. There's a lot of laws when it comes to, like, feeding the house houseless. So they've had issues where, like, they would post up where there's a lot of houseless folks. I guess you could consider them encampments. And in those areas where police have harassed them and gotten in their face about they can't do this and that, but they can't say anything to us because this is private property where they help to pretty much organize and set up the food here. And then that food just gets to be transferred out where it's just a quick drive by. You know, it's like drive by, fooding and. And, you know, and then that helps them. And then with Cafe Namaste, with Sam and her team, it just brings fulfillment to them. What ends up helping with a lot of relationships and just a lot of the people that come here is that there's no intent to gain, in a sense of, like, okay, like, if I do this, and I'm gonna get this and that out of it, like, a lot of people are just genuine where, like, it's Something that they want to do and sometimes coming from the heart and they just want to help. So in a sense with a lot of people that have worked with us that we've noticed that have been consistent and has had a strong relationship with us, that there is a will for them to gain in a sense of that they have fulfilled their duties to, to help. And that's very important for us because that's how we move too. For us, there isn't a gain that's like deeper how, how you understand how people gain from things. It's just we just want to do what we can to help out.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And what about you mentioned like through fiu you were able to meet with icare baby ignited. What do you think they're getting from funding? From helping you with funding?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Same thing. It's just being able to help. You know, they believe in the project, they support it. Eric, which is, I forget his position at the moment, but his whole thing is we're just fans of Green Haven and we just want to continue to help and support however best way we can.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And do you have to fulfill like I know you mentioned earlier, any paperwork that's not really like your area area. And so is there any like contractual obligation that you have to like, do you have to sign any paperwork or promote them in any way on your social media?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: No, fortunately there's no obligations a lot of times. And I feel like for most of the people we've worked with, because I'm trying to think if there has ever been an obligation like that where like if you don't do this and we're not going to work with you kind.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Of thing or even like showing your end of the year.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, no, fortunately we haven't like every so often some of these people ask for, hey, if you can just kind of like write like a quick this, a quick description on how, how our help has benefit you so we can kind of send it off to whoever that would be here and there, but not, not as often where. Like that's a good question because yeah, not a lot of people actually do ask for that. Where it's like they need this and you know, from there then it kind of like questions the, the motivation in the relationship where it's like, okay, well maybe it's not about just actually helping, but no, that isn't the case where it's like legit. People do just want to help.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay. And when you think about, you know, smile, trust or coffee number last day. If you have to rank from 1 to 3, how essential would you say this relationship is for what you're doing?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Definitely a five.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, so three out of three.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, definitely a five. Because, you know, it's. It's just a lot of good people that comes out of it, and that just ends up being a part of the experience. And like I said, for me, that's what matters most is, like, the, like, who you're surrounding yourself by. And, you know, as corny and cheesy as this might sound, it's like, you know, being all about good vibes. It's. That's what I. I'm at, you know, in the sense that life is already difficult as it is. So the fact that, you know, if you're surrounded by people that have ulterior motives or that aren't in it for the love, then this work becomes challenging and not as fulfilling to do it with other people.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay. Okay. And when you can, you think about, like, the different partnerships you've had, the different folks you've collaborated with, and if you're more wanting and willing to speak on that, are there any, like, tensions with any of the partnerships that you have? And if so, how have you resolved those? And it could even be, for example, you know, in the leadership structure, like, how you determine who does what, you know, who takes on what role.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, trying to think how to answer that one about it being, like, too personal or, like, calling anyone out.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Yeah. You don't have to mention any names.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: It's hard to say because, you know, fortunately, there has been some relationship based on some things that have happened, you know, in house that might have affected other relationships, and they haven't necessarily quite been resolved because there might have been some type of biasness or something like that. So. Yeah, I don't. I don't. I think for that one, I don't really have an answer without it being too personal or, like, potentially, like, referencing someone or something, you know, so. Yeah. Okay, that's an interesting question on how people don't really dig that deep when it comes to.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: No, because. Because when we think about the Socioscope project, the goal is to kind of highlight what's working and to also highlight what's not working and potentially how it's been resolved so that other folks, if they do want to go down that path, they have a roadmap. They know what to avoid. Or you can anticipate this type of challenge with maybe this type of funder or this type of partnership or this collaborator, and this is how you could, you know, navigate it.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. I feel you know, even hearing that, like I think it's hard to know how to answer because sometimes some of us might not even have the tools to know how to navigate and alleviate X, Y and Z because there is a lack of understanding. And then for from that lack of understanding there ends up being a lack of tools to know how to use to manage that and to be able to work around it and stuff like that too. So you know, there's a lot, you know, when it comes to managing a non profit that you are still learning. You know, we're learning how we can to innovate, be better, show up more and things like that. So know, it's all learning experience. And I would be very impressed if there's a non profit that has all those answers figured out.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: You know, how about this? What types of tools do you wish were available to manage a non profit? A food sustainability.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, food sustainability.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And is that your legal status?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: No, our legal status would be as a father. 501. C3.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, but you mean as far as like how we're like written. I, I will. I want to say there's maybe like different layers that might be one of them. I think being community and garden, I mean community and garden base is also as well. But yeah, it's a good question. I wish, you know, there's more tools available to kind of like Nani be able to guide people that are non profits especially that are starting on how to like make you know, grant writing easier. Knowing how to apply rules when they're necessary in order to like keep the peace and to keep the, you know, the safety within things. Knowing like how to kind of move about, like being able to ask for things and to reach out to folks in terms of additional help that might be needed that aren't always necessarily available with the team that you have. I think tools like that, you know, that just kind of make things flow better and you know, be able to access more help as well.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: It could be, for example, governmental help.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah. Governmental health up, boots on the ground kind of help. Yeah, it's just different things.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And do you have relationships with governmental or entities in Miami?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Not so much. That has been difficult because there's been in some cases where it feels like the city is not very pro what we're doing. Especially when you're in unfortunately what is considered, you know, a lot of people see Overtown as the quote unquote opportunity zone instead of the, the rich historical black, you know, space that it is. That has always been overtone you know, which is like, you know, during segregation eras where, like, you know, unfortunately, everyone of color was pushed into one space, you know, because it was convenient to keep them here. And then all the wealthy people set up on the beach, but now they're like, figuring out, it's like, okay, that's not as practical as we thought. Now we need to move back, back into where we moved, you know, like, you know, yeah, all the black people there and, and, you know, because of that, it's like now, like I said, they're starting to see it as the quote, unquote, opportunity zone where, like, there's just like a lot of investments and things happening. Redevelopment and, you know, colonization, gentrification, however you want to call it, you know, that's pushing a lot of families out and essentially like a space like here, it would be ideal to build for them. It would be ideal for them to build like a. Another high rise and profit off of that instead of seeing the value of actually having a green space where, you know, everyone likes to talk a good talk about climate, but not enough people are talking a good talk about planting more trees. You know, planting more trees and keeping those spaces that are actually helping to absorb heat.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And does it make your, your operations harder? The gentrification that's happening around?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: It doesn't affect us in that way, but it affects us mentally because you. And unfortunately, it's like something that we kind of foresaw in the beginning where we won't be serving the same families. So it's hard to feel like you're making some kind of impact. But then there's like, other things, things you don't have the answers to, to be able to tackle. Especially in the case of like, where families are getting their rent raised and they can't afford to live there anymore, and then they're getting pushed out and things like that. That does take a mental toll because like I said, you don't see the same families from the beginning to where we are now. And, you know, it's unfortunate because, you know, it's a lot of good people that are just trying to make a living. But I also understand too, if it's people that that also feels the want to leave the neighborhood too, because there also isn't a lot of regulation when it comes to how I mentioned, like, you know, people using drugs and things like that, like out in the open, that, you know, who wants to raise their family around that. Right. And that the fact that the city or law enforcement or anything don't really do what they can to tackle that. We're like, this is not okay. We need to regulate that. This isn't going to fly kind of thing. You know, a lot of it has to be us stepping in and having to even kick people off the sidewalk, being like, hey, don't do that here. Take that somewhere else. You know, you can't stop someone from what they're doing, but you can do what you can from what happens around you.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And then. Final question.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: What would you say are your major expenses?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Major expenses. Besides when it comes to obtaining resources for the season, which would be, you know, things such as getting soil, getting plants, getting soil amendments and this and that. It'll probably be for us, has been pretty much just trying to keep up with, you know, making sure our paperwork is in order, you know, like hiring accountants and people that take care of that and taxes and all that. So. Because that ends up becoming a regular thing where it's like that, there needs to be a pay for that, you know, so.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Just keeping up.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Thank you.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: All right, so can you please tell me about the project or. And your company?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, I am a part of the Green Haven project. We're here based in Miami. Our location is in Overtown. And basically what we do is we create community farms that helps to produce food that's going to serve the community folks that does houseless feedings and anybody in South Florida that needs it. And we pretty much do what we can to teach anyone how to grow their own from various different ways levels, from seed to farming to, you know, planting trees and all that jazz, too. And we also use the space as a resource center to be able to give out various resources, from canned goods back to school products. We even give out toys during Christmas and things like that as some examples.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And what is your name and your role in the company?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so my name is Jorge, and I pretty much function as the growing operationist specialist. I guess I just made that up, but that would pretty be pretty much my role in the org.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, awesome. And how do you contribute to improving the food system?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so we contribute by not only being able to influence people to grow and do their own, so that way it inspires more food to be out there, but we also pretty much make the expenses of food a lot lighter for a lot of people, too, because we have a space where people can utilize to learn how to grow and to produce their own. But then we also have an abundance of food that's available for a lot of people that end up struggling with groceries or things like that, too. Not only that, but none of our. We don't use any harmful or wasteful chemicals on our food. Everything is organic. Like I said, if nature is not eating it, then you probably shouldn't be either. And. Yeah.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And so what would you like to achieve.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: In terms of the Org.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: In terms of the Org, in terms of yourself, too?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, there's a lot I would like to achieve for myself, I think ultimately is to be able to see more people being influenced by what we're doing in terms of them doing it for themselves as well. You know, I would love to see more community gardens, more people coming together to be a part of that experience in that collectiveness and just, you know, just seeing more green coming around. One of the things we can combat, and especially a lot of heat, is we keep planting more trees, keep planting more veggies, keep creating more habitats and all that too.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So what obstacles have you encountered so far and how did you manage those?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: I would say we're still managing a lot of the obstacles that come at us. Now, a lot of it is like time management management when it comes to the actual nonprofit, and just trying to figure out where to best, you know, shift all that. And, you know, we're still figuring it out. There's still a lot to learn. But I think what we've been able to manage more is being able to create a lot of more meaningful relationships. That helps these projects become a lot easier, too.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: When you. You spoke about relationships. So who do you collaborate with on a regular basis?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Yeah, so some of our collaborating teams and groups, some orgs that come to the top of my mind would be the Smile Trust Cafe, Namaste, Citizens for better South Florida, FIU, um, Eye Care BankUnited, just to name a few.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So what advice would you have for people who want to do the same thing that you're doing here at Green Haven?

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: If you don't know where to start, just get out there. Get, like, get. Just get your foot in somewhere. If you know of a space in your city or wherever you're at that has a community garden, go and volunteer. Because, one, you never know who you meet. And then, two, you never know what you learn as well, because this might not be for you, even though you might think in your mind and your heart that this is it. It might not be, but there might be something connected with that that is for you. Because, you know, we have people, even in our team, that they're not growers and they prefer not to be, but they still help out in different ways.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, thank you.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: We are the Green Haven Project and what we do is we help to build community farms in urban areas. Everything that we grow, we give it back to the community for free. And we also use the.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Spaces as.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: A resource center to be able to.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Give various resources.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: From canned goods, non.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Perishables.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Cosmetics, things we.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: Do, school drives, back toy drives, we give a lot.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Of donations and things like that.

entries.speaker: Jorge
entries.text: So.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: We're debriefing the Overtown Green Haven project, which is the usk 68. Thanks la. Can you start by just telling me what. How did you hear about this initiative or how did you find them and what was the experience in getting in touch with them? Were there any difficulties? Was easy. How to go?

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Yes. Hi, Julia. So I found out about Green Haven just by browsing on the Internet, specifically looking for like food sustainability projects in Miami. And when I reached out to them via phone, I think I only did like one outreach and it was very easy to, to get access to them. And also the conversation was very welcoming. He, Jorge, the, the co founder, was very open and willing to, you know, share his experience and specifically knowing that this would help like your research project. So very open and he was very open to, to having an interview and he gave me the times to, to meet up with him. So very easy to do.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great, thanks. Can you give me a. Just a brief overview on what is this project about?

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So this is a project, this is a community garden that was started in 2018 and it's located in like downtown Miami, which for them, they often want to be placed. And I believe they only have one community garden right now. But their purpose was to be placed in areas that are food deserts where people don't necessarily have access to fresh food and organic foods and, and all of that. And so it's in a semi residential neighborhood, but it is a neighborhood that is currently being, or in the process of being gentrified. It is predominantly black and folks of color and folks of lower economic status. And so what this community garden does is to provide access to free food. They also allow their members to, and again, anybody can come in. They also allow their members to come in and plant, grab food. They organize a few different events throughout the year. So they'll have food drives, school drives. So they're not just offering food, but they're also offering like other other essentials that know, families may need to start school or maybe they. That they would need for the holidays. So it almost feels like a community, a communal space for folks to, you know, get replenishment, but to also be replenished themselves because they can participate in the planting of the different legumes and plants and vegetables.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, that's great, thanks. You already touched upon it a little bit. But could you speak a little bit more about, you know, what were the kind of motivations behind getting this project started? What are the kind of values? Maybe also like, if, if. I don't know if Jorge talked to you about the kind of the evolution of the purpose and sort of a bit of their history of how they got where they are. I mean, I guess it's only been around since 2018, you said, but maybe there have been already some kind of key milestones.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Yeah, so when I did ask him about milestones, he didn't necessarily want to touch on like, how much they've grown or like their follower count. For him it was. So the big milestones was to know that people could come in at like any hour of the day that they were open, you know, most days, if not every day, and accessible to all. And it's a place that was reliable for, for all folks to come in. So he didn't necessarily touch on milestones because to him that's not what matters. What matters in terms of like, value is to be acceptable. I mean, accessible to the folks that they're looking to serve. And yeah, they're looking to really access to food to folks who don't necessarily have the means. And especially in a time where specifically and like where they're located, that does become kind of a concern from them. He didn't explicitly say that, but I could gauge that that is a concern because this is a, like, let's just say like a hot territory. They're not necessarily getting the support that they need from local governments from, from the cops. And so a lot of the safeguarding of the garden has to be done for themselves. And at the same time they're worried that they may not be able to serve the folks that they really want to serve, which are folks that are, have a lower economic status. So within the, this gentrification that is going to happen, you know, and their whole purpose was to be in areas that are food deserts. A lot of times with gentrification, like, you get folks of a higher economic status. And so maybe those are not folks that necessarily need the free access to the food because they can afford it. And so I think, I think in general, not just for Green Haven, but for community gardens in food deserts, when gentrification comes about, there tends to be, or there might be like more challenges that they have to face and, and feeding the folks that they're looking to, to access. So for them it's the access to food, but then also being like a, like a safe space for, you know, well, being of the community.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great, Thanks a lot.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Oh, and then I will say so and you, you will probably talk about this when we talk about the different stakeholders, but for them it's not just for them to be accessible to the community, but it could also look like them being a vessel for good. So he talked about, I believe it was perhaps like cafe. I think it was Smile Trust where they often have difficulties because of the laws, accessing houseless folks and feeding them. And so his property, the community garden, is a space where they can eat the that Smile Trust regular would be able to access and feed and meet their mission of feeding folks who don't have food and feeding folks who don't have a home. And so whether that means being a vessel or directly providing to the community, that's one of their core values.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, amazing. Thanks for that rundown. So in terms of their like business model structure, I think I, I did look over the transcript. It seems a bit more informal or at. Yeah, can you tell me a little bit more about like how are they structured? I think I got, I think I remember reading that this isn't like a full time job for, for them, that it's like a, a community pat. Like it's a passion project basically. Is that right? But yeah, tell me a bit more about that.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay. Yeah, so they're a 501c3 organization, specifically like community and garden. And they have a board. At the time when they started, they had about nine members of the board and right now they' down to just four people. And so Jorge being one of the co founders and on the board, he does a lot of the opera. He, he runs a lot of the operations like the in person coordination with the different donors and recipients and he has somebody else on the board who mostly focuses on like the paperwork, the taxes, to be in good standing and also to receive grants and stuff like that. And he did mention that like. Yeah, so right now they're just four people, but there's about five that who are not necessarily super active. So out of the four I mentioned Jorge, I mentioned the person who also does the taxes and the legwork. The two others come by when they can, you know, when they have availability. And also because this is almost like a passion project, no one is paid. He did mention that that's how they're able to sustain this initiative for so long because it's out of the goodness of their heart that they're, you know, doing this.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: How, how big is the, the garden? Like is it? And I mean are they're doing all the planting themselves, right? Or it's like just the turnover of like different people and volunteers or like the community that are coming and planting stuff.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: So I might have Overheard that it's about one acres, so pretty large property. So they don't do the planting themselves. They have a large community of volunteers that come in on a regular basis. They also have for example Cafe Namaste on a monthly basis. They'll have, they'll come in with about like 15 to 20 volunteers that come in and plan and help do the maintenance of the property. It is relatively large and in the summertime that's when they do a lot of their upkeep. So, so you'll notice in the video that you know, it wasn't really, you couldn't necessarily tell like which plants were where there were a lot of weeds. So summer is not the best time for, for collecting the fruits of their labor. And so he did ask, oh if you want to come back in the fall, you know, because that's when you know they would have cleaned up everything and when they'll see growth. But they are heavily supported by the community of volunteers and either direct volunteers or volunteers through some of partnerships.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, okay, great. And, and there was mention of some funders I guess or some like are they applying for grants? Are they? Yeah. I know that you said it wasn't super easy to get information on like their funding financial situation, but is there anything you were able to pull out?

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Yeah, so from my understanding they did get funding. For example, like the community, there's a, there's a cra, which is a redevelopment agency that helped them for example with refensing their property because there were a lot of break ins so they needed to like ramp up the security so they got a funding for that. And then through um, and through FIU they were able to access like they were able to develop relationships with ICARE and like Bank United and those are the folks that provide them additional with additional funding. And it does, it did seem like it was kind of like a one way partnership because I did ask, oh like are there milestones that you have to meet? Are there, is there paperwork that you have to fill out? Like is there a quid pro quo? And he mentioned that. No, it seems like that specifically with the folks at iCare, Bank United, that those are folks that really believe in the project and there's no expectation of any promotion or anything. They'd have to get to give back. So.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, okay, great. Well, maybe we can segue into the kind of stakeholder grid. What would be great is if we could just try and kind of go one by one the different stakeholders that that were mentioned. And I know that this wasn't the easiest exercise, but if you can try and just give as much information as you can about like, what those relationships are, like what they're giving to each other. Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, perfect. So I guess first I would say there's Florida International University, so fiu. And through fiu. Well, okay, let's go first with like Florida International University. So it's really an opportunity for students to learn about like gardening. So that's what they would get, that's what FIU would get in return. And I say that it's because.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: They'Re.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Developing a nursery and he has a nursery that they're going to completely redo and that's going to become an educational center. And I know I'm probably flip flopping here, but. And one of the values that he mentioned is he wants the space to be as practical as possible. Meaning he doesn't want to just have a garden here that just, you know, serves his needs, but it's like, how best can it serve the community? And so with the nursery, which is a partnership with fiu, they're gonna, you know, enhance it so that, you know, students can access the nursery but also learn about gardening. And so that's the type of partnership that they have with fiu. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Great.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Okay, so what do they get from you? They get, I guess a space. So FIU provides the students and with an opportunity to learn about gardening and then they provide the space where, you know, kids can actually. Students can actually go into the nursery to learn and develop their skill sets.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, yeah, that's great.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And it sounded like this was a very important, like essential relationship. And then my understanding too, with, through fiu, they got in contact with I Care and Bank United, who provided the funding.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And so there's no like quid pro quo. There's just like, we believe in the project. Here's some money.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Right. So yeah, we could kind of maybe categorize that stakeholder of like funders as being the I Care Bank United.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Exactly.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Yes. Okay, great. Okay. Who else was there?

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And then we had, for example, Smile Trust. And it sounds like what Smile Trust is getting is a place to feed the homeless because, you know, the laws are pretty strict around like where you can feed and you can't just feed them on the streets, it sounds like. And so they're giving Smile Trust a place to, you know, execute their operation operations. And it's also a safe haven for them relative to like the governmental laws. And I think for, for Green Haven, it meets their core mission, which is to be, you know, a Safe haven for the community, but a place where the community can replenish themselves and get food. I mean, I've talked about the fact that they do food drives, they do school drives, holiday drives, and so it's within that same purpose of where people can get access to food. And so he. They're almost like the middleman.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Smile.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: Trust needs to feed folks, and he provides the space to. For folks to get fed. And so the homeless, the houseless come in there and like families as well. I would say all of these relationships were a three. I did, you know, ask, oh, are there any tensions? But he didn't feel comfortable speaking on that.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: But I'll. I'll talk a little bit after about, like, you know, what they. What it sounds like he would need in general to be able to like, manage those relationships better.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: L.A.
entries.text: And then we have Cafe Namaste. Cafe Namaste is an organization who provides. Like, it's like, you know, they do. You could do yoga. It's like a yoga and wellness organization. So you could do yoga, they offer coffee. But then also every month, you. You as a member of Cafe Namaste, you get an opportunity to go gardening and to volunteer. And so what it. What it. What Caffeine Namaste gets in return is like the fulfillment. Right. There's no intent to gain there. But for Green Haven, they get volunteers, they get people to maintain the property, they got people to plan and to clean up. And so, you know, it's a very. It's a give, give relationship. Yeah. And so they get a lot of break ins, a lot of, you know, people stealing, you know. Yeah, they break in and they steal a couple of things. Right.
